coredata.dc:title: A new nano biosensor for maitotoxin with high sensitivity and selectivity based fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Gholami, Maryam
Salmasi, Marzie Ahari
Sohouli, Esmail
Torabi, Behzad
Sohrabi, Mahmoud Reza
Rahimi-Nasrabadi, Mehdi
coredata.dc:description: Maitotoxin (MTX) is a potent marine toxin with critical LD50, which usually causes poisoning because of eating the contaminated marine crustaceans. This poison is a severe threat to neuronal systems and also disables the heart by increasing the calcium ions flow in the cardiovascular. Therefore, the quantitative determination of MTX by using a fast and cost-effective way is essential. Application of carbon dots in the structure of various sensors for the measurement of biological compounds has been developed due to its easy, rapid and inexpensive synthesis. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were synthesized for the determination of MTX with high sensitivity and selectivity. The structure of CQDs and AuNPs were characterized using SEM (Scanning electron microscopy), TEM (Transmission electron microscopy), EDS (Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy) and FT-IR (Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy) techniques. The Basis of the sensing method is fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) which occurs between energy donor (CQDs) and energy acceptor (AuNPs). Under optimal conditions (excitation wavelength 430 nm and emission wavelength 523 nm), the linear range and detection limit were obtained 1−600 pmol L−1 and 0.3 pmol L-1, respectively. The strong point of this nanosensor is its very fast quenching process. The interference effect of other poisons and some ions on the MTX determination were investigated using CQDs at the constant concentration of MTX50 pmol L-1 and various concentrations of other species. The results show excellent selectivity for the measurement of MTX in the presence of other similar species.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Maitotoxin
Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The general method for determination of MTX
Study of the FRET system formation mechanism
Morphological study of the synthesized AuNPs and CQDs
The absorption and emission spectra of AuNPs and CQDs
Optimization of the AuNPs concentration attached to MTX
Determination of MTX in the real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FRET between CQDs and AuNPs attached to MTX and changing the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength: before the formation of FRET, the maximum fluorescence emission is related to CQDs, and the fluorescence emission spectra of AuNPs attached to MTX are entirely negligible, after FRET system formation, the fluorescence emission of the AuNPs attached to MTX is highly increased and the emission peak of CQDs is disappeared.

The SEM images of AuNPS with magnifications a) × 100 k, b) ×200 k (inset: EDS analysis of AuNPs) c) TEM of AuNPs linked to MTX with magnification ×100 k, SEM images of CQDs with magnifications d) ×20 k, e) ×40 k (inset: EDS analysis of CQDs), f) TEM image of CQDs and g) The FTIR spectra.
UV/Vis absorption spectra of a) AuNPs, b) CQDs and c) Overlapping of the MTX-AuNPs absorption and the CQDs emission spectra.
Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs during the formation of FRET system with AuNPs attached to MTX (50 pmol L−¹) at various concentrations of AuNPs (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, and 3.5 nmol L−¹).
Change in the intensity of the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQD over time, condition: MTX concentration: 50 pmol L−1, AuNPs concentration: 3.5 nmol L-1.
The CQDs emission fluorescence intensity at 523 nm in the presence of 50 pmol L−1 MTX and Au NP (3.5 nmol L-1).
The calibration curve for MTX measurement under the optimum conditions at wavelength 523 nm.
The figure of merit of the suggested method for measurement of MTX.
Measurement of MTX in real samples.

head.ce:title: A new nano biosensor for maitotoxin with high sensitivity and selectivity based fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Chemical Injuries Research Center, Systems Biology and Poisonings Institute, Baqiyatallah University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran.
Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Azad University-North Tehran Branch, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Maitotoxin (MTX) is a potent marine toxin with critical LD50, which usually causes poisoning because of eating the contaminated marine crustaceans. This poison is a severe threat to neuronal systems and also disables the heart by increasing the calcium ions flow in the cardiovascular. Therefore, the quantitative determination of MTX by using a fast and cost-effective way is essential. Application of carbon dots in the structure of various sensors for the measurement of biological compounds has been developed due to its easy, rapid and inexpensive synthesis. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were synthesized for the determination of MTX with high sensitivity and selectivity. The structure of CQDs and AuNPs were characterized using SEM (Scanning electron microscopy), TEM (Transmission electron microscopy), EDS (Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy) and FT-IR (Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy) techniques. The Basis of the sensing method is fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) which occurs between energy donor (CQDs) and energy acceptor (AuNPs). Under optimal conditions (excitation wavelength 430 nm and emission wavelength 523 nm), the linear range and detection limit were obtained 1−600 pmol L−1 and 0.3 pmol L-1, respectively. The strong point of this nanosensor is its very fast quenching process. The interference effect of other poisons and some ions on the MTX determination were investigated using CQDs at the constant concentration of MTX50 pmol L-1 and various concentrations of other species. The results show excellent selectivity for the measurement of MTX in the presence of other similar species.

ce:section.ce:para: Lately, produced toxins from marine natural products such as ciguatoxin, palytoxin and maitotoxin (MTX) have attracted considerable attention of researchers in chemistry, biochemistry and pharmaceutical sciences because of their biological function, toxicity, high molecular weight and complex structures. The structure of MTX was reported for the first time by Murata and Yasumoto in 1993 [1]. This complex compound with an amphiphilic nature has a long alkyl chain containing ether rings, hydroxyl groups and ether sulfate (Fig. S1). Poisoning caused by this substance occurs after eating fish with a sign of dry ice sensation. Also, gastrointestinal and cardiovascular diseases are the effects of the consumption of carnivorous fish infected with this poison [2]. Due to the safety restrictions created by this substance, it is imperative to determine the amount of this poison in marine products. The study of previous resources shows that there are few researches regarding the determination of MTX. Recently, two methods have been developed for the detection of MTX, but these methods do not have good sensitivity and selectivity. Tim Harwood developed an ultra-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) method for the detection of MTX from micro-algal cultures [3]. Also, Bouicha developed the capillary zone electrophoresis (CZE) method with UV detection for MTX determination [4]. Because of the limitations of these methods such as high cost, time-consuming, consumption of large amounts of organic solvents, and low sensitivity, researchers are looking for alternative methods for the analysis of MTX in contaminated fish [5]. One of the novel methods for the determination of desired toxic substances is non-destructive and selective fluorescence sensors [6–10]. There are three different strategies for sensors based on fluorescence, including fluorescence quenching, fluorescence enhancement, and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [6]. In the FRET technique, energy transferring occur from an excited donor fluorophore to an acceptor fluorophore by the coupled transitions involved between the donor emission and acceptor absorbance without emitting a photon. To improve FRET efficiency and sensitivity, new alternatives for both donors and acceptors to replace traditional organic dyes were introduced. The application of carbon nanostructures is common in fluorescence systems. The first published work containing these nanoparticles was about carbon nanotube (CNT). This paper introduced the purification of fluorescent single-walled CNT fragments that have special size with high fluorescent emission and quantum yields [11]. After that, a series of carbon nanoparticles have been used in FRET assays. Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and rare earth doped upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) are used as FRET donors. Some of the common FRET acceptors are gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and graphene oxide (GO). Using of NPs providing better efficiency, stability and performance for fluorescence-based sensors [7]. Quantum-dot-(QDs)-based fluorescence methods have attracted more attention because of their simplicity, convenience and high sensitivity which is related to the selective response of luminescence quenching for target analytes [12–14]. QDs as semiconductor nanocrystals with a limited size have electro-optical properties between the molecular semiconductors [15]. Because of QDs' low toxicity, stable photoluminescence properties, good biocompatibility, better surface adhesion, good performance in cellular imaging, it is used in optoelectronic devices, sensors and biochemical imaging as a novel green material [16,17]. Several QDs probes have been developed for measurement and detection of different biological species. There are several studies using CQDs in FRET phenomena with different analytes, including ions, toxins, pesticides [18–21]. GQDs for detection of ascorbic acid with a detection limit of 94 nmol L−1 [22] and propylene/graphene quantum dots (PPy/GQDs) for dopamine (DA) detection with a detection limit of 10 pmol L−1 have been used previously [23]. In recent years, fluorescent carbon CQDs have attracted great attention due to their excellent structural and optical properties [24]. Various techniques have been used for the synthesis of CQDs, such as hydrothermal [25,26], microwave methods [27] and so on [28]. They also have been produced from different carbon precursors such as glycerol [29] and citric acid [30]. AuNPs as an important type of metal nanoparticles have been applied in a wide range of fields such as catalysis, sensors, biosensors and biomedicines due to their special physical and chemical properties [20]. The unique electrochemical and optical properties of these nanoparticles which are originated from their free electrons resonance after excitation (enhancement of FRET in the presence of plasmonic metal as localized surface plasmon resonance) are changed with shape, size, geometry, surface-to-volume ratio and its aggregation states [31–35]. The conjugation of different functional groups of biomolecules (such as MTX) to AuNPs is necessary for their stability and biocompatibility and development of their application fields. In the present work, a simple method with high efficiency was applied for the fabrication of low-cost CQDs from primary biological species of biochar as a carbonic precursor. Considering the low detection limit and the appropriate cost of these CQDs based sensors, a CQDs based-new fluorescent sensor has been successfully developed for analyzing of MTX attached to AuNPs (MTX/AuNPs) in marine products.

Nitric acid, sodium hydroxide, tri-sodium citrate, phosphorus pentachloride, Tetrachloroauric acid (HAucl4), 1,4-dioxane, tributylphosphine, calcium chloride, magnesium chloride, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, iron (II) chloride, formalin, sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate and disodium phosphate were purchased from Merck (http://www.merck.com) or Sigma-Aldrich (https://www.istgah.com).

In the present work, the fluorescence spectra and intensity measurements were done by using RF-3501 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp and 1 cm quartz cell. An UV–vis spectrophotometer V-530 Corporation (Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with a deuterium lamp was used. FT-IR (Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy) spectra (4000–400 cm_1) were recorded with a Bruker equipment model FT/IR-410 (JASCO, Japan). A Sigma, Vertex field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (Germany) coupled with Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (Oxford Instruments, England) was used for close-view imaging of materials. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) instruments (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany) was used and centrifugation was performed by using a Sigma 3 K 15 centrifuge.

CQDs were synthesized using biochar according to the method reported by Ray et al. [29]. Briefly, biochar was refluxed in the nitric acid (5 mol L1) solution for 20 h under vigorous stirring by using a magnetic stirrer at room temperature. Subsequently, the suspension was neutralized by sodium hydroxide and then ultracentrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The product was dialyzed against distilled water for 72 h. Dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off 1000 Da) was used in the purification of the CQDs [36]. The highly fluorescent CQDs were transferred to the flask diluted to 50 ml with water and used for experiments.

The AuNPs were prepared by the reduction of HAuCl4 in the aqueous solution using trisodium citrate, according to the previously reported method [37]. Briefly, 5 ml of the aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (5 mmol L−1) was added to 91 ml of double-distilled water. The solution was heated until boiling and then 4 ml of a 1% mass fraction of sodium tetracitrate anhydrous was added to the boiling solution with vigorous magnetic stirring. After 30 min of reaction (the color of the solution was changed from bright yellow to ruby red), the dispersed nanoparticles were cooled until room temperature. Purification of AuNPs was performed by centrifugation operating at 7000 g for 20 min which recovers of gold nanoparticles without detectable impurities [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The general method for determination of MTX
ce:section.ce:para: To establish a FRET system between AuNPs attached to MTX and CQDs, 1 ml of prepared CQDs suspension and 2 ml of AuNPs (3.5 nM) were added to a 5 ml round-bottom flask. Then a suitable amount of MTX standard or sample solution in the range of calibration curve was added to the prepared mixture and then diluted with distilled water to 5 mL. The FRET system was obtained after 10 min of slow stirring. After incubation for 10 min at ambient temperature, the intensity of fluorescence was recorded at the maximum wavelength of 523 nm with an excitation wavelength of 430 nm.

Before analyzing of MTX in fish samples, the samples were treated in several steps. The abdomen of each fish was opened after removing the fins and tail with a clean blade, it was immediately immersed into formalin 10 % stabilizer buffer solution 4 °C to penetrate into the samples tissue. The stabilizer buffer solution prevents analyte degradation originated from changing pH or temperature. To prepare 1 L of formalin buffer, 4 g of sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate and 6.5 g of disodium phosphate were added to 100 ml of formaldehyde and 900 ml of distilled water. Then the samples packed in suitable polymer containers and transferred to the sample analysis place in the ice flask. After transferring the collected samples to the laboratory, they were removed from the buffer and then 5 ml of hexane was added on 5 g of the chopping sample pieces and the mixture was stirred for 10 min to obtain a completely homogenized mixture. The resulting mixture was centrifuged for 5 min to extract the analyte from the tissue sample. The resulting solution was filtered through the 0.45 μm filter and was used in real sample analysis. The amount of MTX in real samples was determined using the standard addition method and the accuracy of the method was investigated by spiking of real samples with different concentrations of MTX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the FRET system formation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In fluorescence studies based on the FRET process, it is important to select suitable acceptor and donor chromophores. CQDs as fluorescent carbon nanoparticles in the role of a donor agent have maximum fluorescence emission in wavelength of 527 nm. The CQDs made from natural sources (biochar) are considered to be completely safe for various applications in biological samples. There is almost no identified toxicity in these green carbon nanoparticles. An appropriate FRET process between CQDs and an acceptor reagent can be carried out when there is an appropriate overlap between the absorption and emission spectra of acceptor and donor materials. In this work, the synthesized AuNPs have a maximum absorbent in wavelength of 550 nm that acts as a fluorescence receptor in the FRET system after binding to MTX. One of the highlighted properties of AuNPs is their enzymatic mimicking activity in biology which can help the researchers to use these nanoparticles as artificial enzymes in some applications such as biosensors, drug delivery, bio-imaging, detection and clinical studying [39–41]. Due to the enzymatic properties and the applicability of AuNPs in the detection of MTX and also preventing the accumulation of nanoparticles, the surface of AuNPs can be easily attached to biological MTX ligands.

In this work, to form the FRET system, two acceptor and donor molecules must be moved closer together. The ligand has been used to increase the selectivity and furthermore to establish better contact between the Au NPs and CQDs, by hindering the aggregation of Au NPs. The ligands with a thiol group (-SH) like MTX provide a strong covalent bond with AuNPs. In the first step, the OSO3 − group in used ligand in this system (e.g. MTX) should be converted to –SH group by chemical reduction. To access the chemical reduction of MTX, this poison is first exposed to the phosphorus pentachloride (PCl5) for 4 h at 20 °C. In the next step, the dioxane and tetra butyl phosphine reagents were used to reduce sulfur dioxide to the thiol group. This reaction is done at room temperature for 15 h. In the next step, with increasing the AuNPs concentration in the solution containing MTX, a strong bond is formed between the thiol group of MTX and nanoparticles surface and the inorganic AuNPs become stable. The schematic of this process is shown in Fig. S2.

Before approaching and adding AuNPs to MTX, fluorescence emission is only related to CQDs dots so that the fluorescence emission of AuNPs attached to MTX is completely negligible (Scheme 1 ). Following the attachment of aggregated particles to MTX, the fluorescence emission of AuNPs attached to MTX is highly increased due to the formation of the FRET system, while the emission of CQDs reaches to zero. The FRET process between the CQDs and the AuNPs attached to the MTX is shown in a schematic form (Scheme 1). As a result of sandwich assay structure formation of CQDs–MTX–AuNPs, CQDs were brought close to AuNPs surface and fluorescence quenching of CQDs and FRET system emission were realized. For the establishment of the FRET system between AuNPs attached to MTX and CQDs, the optimal concentrations of the AuNPs attached to MTX, and CQDs were used. After 10 min, the fluorescence emission of CQDs is effectively quenched through the formation of the FRET system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological study of the synthesized AuNPs and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FE-SEM images of synthesized AuNPs are shown in Fig. 1 a and b. The size distribution of AuNPs is almost identical and relatively uniform which indicates the suitability of the nucleation process. However, agglomeration is observed in some parts indicating the longer growth stage. The FE-SEM images of the synthesized CQDs are presented in Fig. 1d and e. In spite of the agglomeration of CQDs in some parts, they are similar to those reported in other references [29], which confirms the successful synthesis of CQDs.

The tendency bind of AuNPs to thiol groups of MTX may be concluded from the TEM image of AuNPs attached to MTX (Fig. 1c). As can be seen, increasing the diameter of nanoparticles and creating shadows at the edges of the modified nanoparticles is readily detectable in the image due to the coating of them by MTX. Nanoparticles covered by MTX are dispersed as spherical and regular particles. Fig. 1g shows the FTIR spectra of the biochar, CQDs, MTX, AuNPs-MTX and MTX (after the reduction of the OSO3 −to –SH group). FTIR is a suitable method in determining the functional groups and confirming the presence of a substance [42,43]. The FTIR spectrum of the biochar sample shows a broad peak at 3420 cm−1which corresponds to the OH bond stretching of the alcoholic and phenolic hydroxyl groups. The characteristic CH stretching vibration of the alkyl structure of an aliphatic group can be seen at 2930 cm−1. The peak at 1790 cm−1 represents the stretching of carbonyl bonds (CO) of the carboxylic groups or conjugated ketone. The stretching vibrations of the aliphatic –C = C– appear at 1446 cm−1 [44]. The spectrum of CQDs shows functional groups CO at the vibrational frequency of 1269 cm-1, the ether groups of COC in the region of 1427 cm-1, the groups of the double-carbon bonds related to the graphite structure of the CQDs (CC) in the 1631 cm-1 region, the carbonyl groups related to the functional groups of carboxylic acid in the vibrational region of 1739 cm-1, the tensile vibrations of C–H in the region of 3100 cm-1, as well as the broad peaks in the 3480 cm-1 region for OH-functional groups which indicate the proper synthesis of these CQDs. The FTIR spectrum of MTX include CH stretching band at 745 cm-1 and 2980 cm-1, = C–H tensile band at 3069 cm-1, CC tensile bond at 1530 cm-1, the very broad tensile band of OH-functional groups at 1291 cm-1, 2959 cm-1 and 3650−3290 cm-1, O–S tensile band at 781 cm-1, COC broad tensile band at 1190 cm-1, and the tensile bond SO at 1450 cm-1.The FTIR spectrum of MTX is observed after the reduction of the functional group OSO3 − to –SH. In this spectrum, the tensile bands correspond to SO and SO appeared in the structure of MTX was removed and a tensile band of SH has appeared at 1769 cm-1. The stretching bands of CC are shifted from 1530 cm−1 to 1603 cm−1, the COC band from 1190 cm
−1 to 1210 cm−1, the OH band from 2959 cm−1 to 2998 cm−1 and 3650−3290 cm−1 to 3805−3430 cm−1. To investigate the binding of the thiol group (-SH) in recovered MTX to AuNPs, the FTIR spectra of AuNPs attached to the MTX was also investigated. The band at 762 cm-1 is assigned to the covalent reaction of -SH group with Au nanoparticles [45]. In this structure, the positions of peaks corresponded to the functional groups of MTX (after the reduction of the OSO3 −to –SH group) have been shifted slightly to the left after binding the AuNPs to the sulfur. In addition, the decrease in the peak intensity of the thiol group indicates the binding of AuNPs to this functional group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The absorption and emission spectra of AuNPs and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a shows the absorption spectrum of AuNPs before binding to MTX. In this figure, a broad absorption spectrum is observed in the wavelength range of 500–600 nm for this structure, with a maximum wavelength of about 550 nm. After binding the MTX with the nanoparticles, the maximum absorption wavelength is shifted to about 523 nm (Fig. 2c). The absorption spectra of CQDs are also observed in Fig. 2b. The CQDs display excitation wavelength-dependent emission properties, that is, the maximum fluorescence emission shifts from 450 to 530 when the excitation wavelengths change from 360 to 460 nm. The maximum fluorescence emission is about 523 nm when excited at 430 nm (Fig. 2c). A FRET system is effective when the maximum emission wavelength of the donor is close to the maximum absorption wavelength of an acceptor [6,7]. The absorption and emission spectra of the corresponding particles are shown in Fig. 2c. As seen in the figure, there is a significant overlap between the absorption spectrum of AuNPs and the emission spectrum of the CQDs. Therefore, suitable fluorescent substrates or fluorescent sensors is occurred based on the high-performance fluorescence energy transfer technique which is based on the emission of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the AuNPs concentration attached to MTX
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal ratio of AuNP to CQDs is defined in such a way that (i) total MTX molecules attached to the AuNPs are formed the FRET system with CQDs and (ii) free CQDs are not present in the solution. In this case, the fluorescence emission of CQDs is quenched well and also, the concentration of AuNPs attached to the MTX in the solution remains unchanged. If the concentration of CQDs is more than the desired concentration, in addition to attaching of MTX molecules to AuNPs-CQDs, a small amount of CQDs remains free in the solution and the emission spectrum of the particles is not quenched due to the signal appeared from control solution. Different concentrations of AuNPs attached to the MTX were investigated to measure MTX through the formation of the FRET system with CQDs. At this stage, the AuNPs attached to the MTX with the concentration range of 0.5–3.5 nmol L−¹were added to the FRET system containing CQDs. Then the emission spectrum of the CQDs was studied by increasing the concentration from 0.5–3.5 nmol L−1. As shown in Fig. 3 with an increase in the AuNPs concentration attached to MTX into a solution containing CQDs, the intensity of its fluorescence emission decreases due to the formation of the FRET system and the FRET process is progressed. Based on these observations, the optimum concentration of the AuNPs attached to MTX was obtained 3.5 nmol L-1.

To investigate the kinetics of FRET system formation after adding 3.5 nmol L−1 of AuNPs attached to MTX-CQDs, optimization of the time was performed. The time interval from 1−10 min was examined and the intensity of fluorescence emission was measured every minute. After 10 min of slow stirring, the FRET system was obtained and the substances CQDs were effectively quenched. Fig. 4 shows the change in the intensity of the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs over time. Observations show that by increasing the time from 1−10 min, the intensity of the fluorescence emission reaches the minimum value due to the formation of the FRET system. Hence, 10 min was selected as the optimal time for the next levels.

The various concentration of CQDs were used to determine the appropriate amount of CQDs for the establishment of the FRET system with nanoparticles attached to MTX. Here, in each step, the nanoparticles with 3.5 nmol L−1concentration interact with different concentrations of CQD. As shown in Fig. 5 with an increase in the concentration of CQDs, the fluorescence emission spectrum of QDs increases. At lower concentrations (optimal value = 1.0 nM), all of the fluorescence emission of CQDs is absorbed by AuNPs and the final emission intensity of the fluorescence of CQDs decreases.

The analytical performance of the method was investigated by determination of linear dynamic range, the square of the correlation coefficient, detection limit (LOD), the limits of quantification (LOQ) and relative standard deviations for the analysis of MTX at optimum conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 fluorescence intensity increases linearly by increasing the MTX concentration. Under optimal conditions, the linear region was obtained in the concentration range from 1 to 600 pmol L−¹ (R2 = 0.9972). The LOD is the concentration which signal of analytes equal to three times of the standard deviation signal for the control sample or in other words, a concentration in which the signal to noise ratio is about 3 fold (S/N = 3). To determine of LOD, the determination of a repetitive signal of the control sample and its standard deviation was used. In this experiment, the LOD for MTX was calculated as 0.3 pmol L−1. The quantitation limit is the concentration of analyte which the signal to noise ratio is about 10 fold (S/N = 10). The value of LOQ was obtained 1 pmol L−1. The relative standard deviation which is a measure of the precision of the method was calculated from the repetitive determinations and the obtained figure of merits for the proposed method and a comparison with other methods is shown in Table 1 .

The ultimate aim of this method is to measure MTX in real samples. Therefore, the effect of associated species such as other poisons, amino acids, and cations, which likely interferes with the determination of the final signal of the analyte, were investigated. To examine the effect of the associated species on the response of MTX, the concentration of 0.1 μmol L−¹ of interfering species was added to the samples containing 50 pmol L-1 of MTX, and the determinations were carried out in the presence of these associated species. Interfering species selected were likely to interact with AuNPs and interfere with the FRET system. The obtained results in Fig. S3 shows that the effect interfering species in real samples is negligible on the determination of MTX. The obtained results in Fig. S3 shows that the effect interfering species in real samples is negligible on the determination of MTX. In the presence of different compounds at the above concentration, deviation in signal was lower than 5%, so it can be concluded that the developed sensor has good selectivity in addition to good sensitivity for the determination of MTX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MTX in the real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, MTX was measured in the samples of fish caught from southern Iran. The results of real samples analysis are presented in Table 2 . The amount of MTX in real samples was obtained with high accuracy by using the calibration curve equation and fluorescence intensity resulted from the FRET system. The wide linear range, excellent sensitivity and excellent selectivity in addition to low detection limits are the most important advantages of the prepared sensor which makes it an ideal sensor for MTX determination in the real samples.

In this work, an effective FRET between CQDs (as a donor) and AuNPs (as acceptor) were resulted from the spectral overlapping of the emission spectra of them. The interaction between MTX with AuNPs has an increasing effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs system, and this effect is the basis of the sensitive determination of the analyte. Determination using this method is simple and low cost and can be easily performed by a spectrofluorometric device. It can be concluded that the proposed method has excellent sensitivity and selectivity for measuring MTX due to the low detection limit, wide linear range and no interference from studied organic and inorganic species.
Author’s statement

Mrs. Maryam Gholami, Dr. Marzieh Ahari Salmasi, Mr. Esmail Sohouli, synthesis, characterize carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles also acquire various fluorescence data, Dr. Behzad Torabi edited the manuscript and Prof. Mehdi Rahimi-Nasrabadi and Dr.Mahmood Reza Sohrabi propose the research, provide all facility and investigate the different data and furthermore edited the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support of the Azad University-North Tehran Branch.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112523.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Neurotoxic marine poisoning
Lancet Neurol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.K. Isbister, M.C. Kiernan, "Neurotoxic marine poisoning", The Lancet Neurology, 4, 2005 PP 219-228

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The structure elucidation and biological activities of high molecular weight algal toxins: maitotoxin, prymnesins and zooxanthellatoxins"
Nat. Prod. Rep.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Murata, T. Yasumoto, "The structure elucidation and biological activities of high molecular weight algal toxins: maitotoxin, prymnesins and zooxanthellatoxins", Natural product reports, 17, 2000 PP 293-314

Marine and Fresh-Water Harmful Algae

Toxicon
